Summary: The study develops and validates a machine learning model using 18F-FDG PET/CT radiomics and clinical features to predict the presence of micropapillary and solid (MP/S) components in lung adenocarcinoma. The combined PET/CT radiomics-clinical model demonstrates superior performance in distinguishing patients with MP/S components, potentially aiding in personalized treatment and surveillance strategies.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient enrollment, aiming to create a representative study population.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is based on pathological analysis according to the 2011 IASLC/ATS/ERS classification of lung adenocarcinoma, which is a well-established standard.

Item4.reason: The study is a single-center retrospective study.

Item5.reason: 18F-FDG PET/CT is a clinically translatable imaging technique widely used for lung cancer diagnosis, staging, and prognosis.

Item6.reason: The study reports the imaging protocol with acquisition parameters for both CT and PET scans.

Item7.reason: The study mentions that the operation was done within 2 weeks after the PET/CT scan, which is an appropriate interval to validate the presence or absence of target conditions at the moment of diagnostic imaging exams.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of the tumor volume of interest (VOI) on PET/CT images.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed semi-automatically by two experienced nuclear medicine physicians, indicating human intervention.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including the software used (ITK-SNAP), the physicians involved, and the threshold used for delineation (40% SUVmax).

Item9.reason: The segmentation was semi-automatic, not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: Two readers delineated the boundaries of the tumor to define the VOI. Therefore, the final segmentation in the test set was not produced by a single reader or an automated tool.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts 1688 radiomics features from each VOI, which are hand-crafted features.

Item11.reason: The study mentions resampling the isotropic voxel into 1x1x1 mm with linear interpolation, which is an appropriate preprocessing technique. It also mentions the lung window settings for CT segmentation.

Item12.reason: The study used the pyradiomics software for feature extraction.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (radiomics features from PET and CT images) and mentions the use of Pyradiomics software. It also specifies the version number.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomics features and clinical variables organized in tables.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. It uses traditional machine learning methods with hand-crafted radiomic features.

Item14.reason: The study performs a Mann-Whitney U validation and Pearson correlation analysis to remove redundant features.

Item15.reason: The study uses LASSO algorithm for feature selection, which is a dimensionality reduction technique.

Item16.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size or provide statistical justification.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study divides the patients into training, test cohort 1, and test cohort 2 randomly. However, it is not explicitly mentioned whether the data split was done at the patient level or whether a fixed random seed was used.

Item19.reason: The study considers clinical variables such as gender, age, smoking, TNM stage, nodule type, tumor size, SUVmax, SUVavg, and MTV as potential confounding factors and analyzes their influence.

Item20.reason: The study uses AUC for ROC curves to assess predictive performance and performs calibration curves. It also uses decision curve analysis (DCA) to evaluate clinical practicability.

Item21.reason: The study does not report uncertainty measures such as confidence intervals or standard deviations for the performance metrics.

Item22.reason: The study performs Hosmer-Lemeshow validation and calibration curves to analyze the calibration of the nomogram.

Item23.reason: The study uses both PET and CT radiomics features and does not compare the performance of uni-parametric imaging (PET or CT alone) with the combined approach to justify the need for multi-parametric imaging.

Item24.reason: The study does not compare the radiomics-based model with a non-radiomic method representative of clinical practice. It only compares the combined model with PET and CT radiomics models.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the model with a simple baseline reference model or justify the use of machine learning methods with proof of increased performance compared to simpler alternatives.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on two independent test cohorts sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The study does not perform external testing with independent data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study includes an "Availability of data and materials" section stating that the datasets analyzed are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention sharing the scripts related to automatic segmentation and/or modeling with the public.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention sharing the final model in the form of a raw model file or as a ready-to-use system.
